trialDesign.fullName: Coadministered Cagrilintide and Semaglutide in Adults with Overweight or Obesity
trialDesign.overview: ## BACKGROUND Semaglutide at a dose of 2.4 mg has established weight-loss and cardiovascular benefits, and cagrilintide at a dose of 2.4 mg has shown promising results in early-phase trials; the efficacy of the combination (known as CagriSema) on weight loss in persons with either overweight and coexisting conditions or obesity is unknown.
trialDesign.design: In a phase 3a, 68-week, multicenter, double-blind, placebo-controlled and active-controlled trial, we enrolled adults without diabetes who had a body-mass index (BMI); the weight in kilograms divided by the square of the height in meters) of 30 or higher or a BMI of 27 or higher with at least one ob
trialDesign.population: enrolled adults without diabetes who had a body-mass index (BMI); the weight in kilograms divided by the square of the height in meters) of 30 or higher or a BMI of 27 or higher with at least one obesity-related complication.

headings.background: Semaglutide at a dose of 2.4 mg has established weight-loss and cardiovascular benefits, and cagrilintide at a dose of 2.4 mg has shown promising results in early-phase trials; the efficacy of the combination (known as CagriSema) on weight loss in persons with either overweight and coexisting conditions or obesity is unknown.

headings.design: In a phase 3a, 68-week, multicenter, double-blind, placebo-controlled and active-controlled trial, we enrolled adults without diabetes who had a body-mass index (BMI); the weight in kilograms divided by the square of the height in meters) of 30 or higher or a BMI of 27 or higher with at least one ob
headings.population: enrolled adults without diabetes who had a body-mass index (BMI); the weight in kilograms divided by the square of the height in meters) of 30 or higher or a BMI of 27 or higher with at least one obesity-related complication.

trialSummary.conclusion: Cagrilintide–semaglutide provided significant and clinically relevant body-weight reductions in adults with overweight or obesity, as compared with placebo. (Funded by Novo Nordisk; REDEFINE 1 ClinicalTrials.gov number, NCT05567796.) The authors' full names, academic degrees, and affiliations are listed at the end of the article. Dr. Garvey can be contacted at garveyt@uab.edu or at the Department of Nutrition Sciences, University of Alabama at Birmingham, 1675 University Blvd., Birmingham, AL 35294-3360. A list of investigators in the REDEFINE 1 trial is provided in the Supplementary Appendix,

items.question: How does the process described in reference 2 relate to REDEFINE 1?
items.answer: Pharmacotherapy for obesity, as an adjunct to lifestyle intervention, may provide sustained weight reduction, improve health, and ameliorate obesity-related complications. Semaglutide, a glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist, is approved at a dose of 2.4 mg once weekly, administered subcutaneously, for long-term weight management and cardiovascular risk reduction. The approval of semaglutide was based on findings from the global phase 3 STEP (Semaglutide Treatment Effect in People with Obesity) program and the SELECT (Semaglutide Effects on Cardiovascular Outcomes in People with Overweight or Obesity) trial, which showed clinically significant bodyweight reductions of 9.6 to 17.4% in persons with overweight or obesity with or without diabetes, as well as reductions in cardiometabolic risk factors and in cardiovascular events.
items.reference: Ref 2: "Pharmacotherapy for obesity, as an adjunct to lifestyle intervention, may provide sustained weight reduction, improve health, and ameliorate obesity-related com..." — Garvey WT, Mechanick JI, Brett EM, et al. American Association of Clinical Endocrinologists and American College of Endo

items.question: Why is the following clinically relevant in REDEFINE 1? Context: Amylin is a satiety hormone involved in the central regulation of food intake, body weight, and glycemia. Cagrilintide i…
items.answer: Amylin is a satiety hormone involved in the central regulation of food intake, body weight, and glycemia. Cagrilintide is a long-acting, human amylin analogue, with affinity for three amylin receptors (AMY1R, AMY2R, and AMY3R) and a calcitonin receptor. Cagrilintide affects appetite regulation through direct effects in the brain. In a phase 2 trial, cagrilintide at a dose of 2.4 mg led to a 9.7% weight reduction by week 26. Because the combination of therapies with complementary but distinct mechanisms of action may lead to greater weight loss than individual medications, a phase 1b trial was developed to investigate the combination of once-weekly semaglutide at a dose of 2.4 mg and cagrilintide at a dose of 2.4 mg; a weight reduction of -17.1% over 20 weeks was observed, with an acceptable safety profile. We now provide the efficacy and safety results of a phase 3a, 68-week trial of cagrilintide–semaglutide (known as CagriSema) for the treatment of persons with obesity or with overweight and at least one coexisting condition

Because the combination of therapies with complementary but distinct mechanisms of action may lead to greater weight loss than individual medications, a phase 1b trial was developed to investigate the combination of once-weekly semaglutide at a dose of 2.4 mg and cagrilintide at a dose of 2.4 mg; a weight reduction of -17.1% over 20 weeks was observed, with an acceptable safety profile. We now provide the efficacy and safety results of a phase 3a, 68-week trial of cagrilintide–semaglutide (known as CagriSema) for the treatment of persons with obesity or with overweight and at least one coexisting condition

items.question: Why is the following clinically relevant in REDEFINE 1? Context: Our trial was conducted in accordance with the principles of the Declaration of Helsinki and Good Clinical Practice guid…
items.answer: Our trial was conducted in accordance with the principles of the Declaration of Helsinki and Good Clinical Practice guidelines. The sponsor (Novo Nordisk) was responsible for trial design, preparation of the protocol (available at NEJM.org) and statistical analysis plan, site monitoring, and statistical analyses. All the authors had full access to the trial data and analysis, interpreted the aggregate data, participated in drafting the manuscript, and vouch for the accuracy and completeness of the data and for the fidelity of the trial to the protocol. Medical writing support, including the writing of the first draft of the manuscript under the direction of the authors, was provided by and funded by Novo Nordisk, in accordance with Good Publication Practice guidelines. The investigators were responsible for trial-related medical decisions and data collection. An extension phase (ClinicalTrials.gov number, NCT06780449) is ongoing

items.question: How does the process described in reference 20 relate to REDEFINE 1?
items.answer: We calculated that a sample size of 3400 would provide an effective power of 99% for the coprimary and confirmatory secondary end points, which were tested in a prespecified hierarchical order. All efficacy end points were analyzed in the full analysis population (all the participants who underwent randomization, in accordance with the intention-to-treat principle), and safety end points in the safety analysis population (all the participants who underwent randomization and received 1 dose of an active drug or placebo). Two observation periods were defined for the efficacy end points: the in-trial period was defined as the time from randomization to the last contact with the trial site regardless of discontinuation of the active drug or placebo or receipt of rescue intervention, and the on-treatment period was defined as the time from the first administration of the active drug or placebo to the first discontinuation (i.e., the date when no trial product had been administered for 14 days) without rescue intervention (i.e., other obesity medication or bariatric surgery). Two estimands were used to assess efficacy. In our trial, the treatment-policy estimand was used to assess the effects regardless of discontinuation of the drug or placebo or receipt of rescue intervention (consistent with the intention-to-treat principle) and was based on data from the in-trial period, and the trial-product estimand was used to assess the effects if the trial products were taken as intended (including any of the approved doses) and was based on data from the on-treatment period; all reported results are for the treatment-policy estimand unless stated otherwise. Additional details regarding the estimands, analysis methods, and modeling for the continuous and binary end points are provided in

items.question: How does the process described in reference 21 relate to REDEFINE 1?
items.answer: From November 1, 2022, to June 28, 2023, a total of 3417 participants underwent randomization; 2108 participants were randomly assigned to receive cagrilintide–semaglutide, 302 to receive semaglutide alone, 302 to receive cagrilintide alone, and 705 to receive placebo (Fig. S2). A majority of the participants were women (67.6%) and White (72.0%); the mean age was 47.0 years. At baseline, the mean body weight was 106.9 kg, the mean BMI was 37.9, and the mean waist circumference was 114.7 cm (Table 1). Dyslipidemia and hypertension were the most common obesity-related complications, and 32.1% of the population had prediabetes. Overall, the baseline characteristics of the participants were similar across the groups (Table 1 and Table S1). The representativeness of the trial population and the generalizability of our findings are presented in Table S2. The percentage of participants still receiving the drug or placebo at week 68 was 88.2% in the cagrilintide–semaglutide group, 91.4% in the semaglutide group, 87.7% in the cagrilintide group, and 75.9% in the placebo group. At week 68, the percentage of participants receiving the maximum dose of the drug or placebo was 57.4% in the cagrilintide–semaglutide group,
items.reference: Ref 21: "From November 1, 2022, to June 28, 2023, a total of 3417 participants underwent randomization; 2108 participants were randomly assigned to receive cagrilintide–..." — American Diabetes Association Professional Practice Committee. Diagnosis and classification of diabetes: Standards of Ca

items.question: Which finding is supported by reference 22 in REDEFINE 1?
items.explanation: In this trial, treatment with cagrilintide–semaglutide resulted in a significantly greater reduction in body weight than semaglutide, cagrilintide, or placebo. In addition, participants receiving cagrilintide–semaglutide were significantly more likely than those receiving placebo to reach weight-loss targets of 5% or more, 20% or more, 25% or more, and 30% or more. The mean weight reduction observed with cagrilintide–semaglutide is in the highest range of that observed with existing weight-loss interventions. We posit that these effects on body weight probably result from the complementary effect of the two molecules on appetite regulation through direct actions in brain regions known to be involved in hedonic and homeostatic appetite regulation, such as the hypothalamus, hindbrain, and septum.

items.question: Which finding is supported by reference 33 in REDEFINE 1?
items.explanation: the trial-product estimand (i.e., when participants were treated as intended). Statistical superiority of cagrilintide–semaglutide over placebo was observed for all confirmatory secondary end points in the testing hierarchy, including systolic blood pressure and waist circumference; improvements in lipid levels were also observed. The benefits of cagrilintide–semaglutide as compared with placebo in reducing glycated hemoglobin levels were reflected by the percentage of participants with prediabetes who attained normoglycemia in these groups (88% vs. 32%). Improvements in blood pressure generally occurred early in the trial, when maximum weight loss had not yet been achieved, and were sustained through week 68 in participants receiving cagrilintide–semaglutide; improvements of this magnitude may be considered to be similar to those achieved with antihypertensive medications in clinical trials. Favorable changes in body composition were observed in the participants receiving cagrilintide–semaglutide; specifically, greater reductions in fat mass (67%) than lean soft-tissue mass (33%) and improvement in the ratio of fat to lean soft-tissue mass were noted and are representative of results observed in other trials assessing obesity medications, although the magnitude of the reduction in lean soft-tissue mass warrants further investigation. Significantly better participant-reported physical function outcomes were observed with cagrilintide–semaglutide than with placebo and were greater than those observed previously with semaglutide as compared with placebo.
items.reference: Ref 33: "the trial-product estimand (i.e., when participants were treated as intended). Statistical superiority of cagrilintide–semaglutide over placebo was observed for..." — Guerrero-García C, Rubio-Guerra AF. Combination therapy in the treatment of hypertension. items.question: Why is the following clinically relevant in REDEFINE 1? Context: The safety profile of cagrilintide–semaglutide was consistent with that for the GLP-1 receptor agonist class of weight-l…
items.answer: The safety profile of cagrilintide–semaglutide was consistent with that for the GLP-1 receptor agonist class of weight-loss agents and previous safety data for cagrilintide. Gastrointestinal adverse events occurred less frequently in the cagrilintide group than in the semaglutide and cagrilintide–semaglutide groups. Treatment discontinuation due to adverse events was similar to that in other studies of incretin-based therapies. The efficacy and safety of cagrilintide–semaglutide in persons with type 2 diabetes and overweight or obesity are being assessed in the REDEFINE 2 trial

Gastrointestinal adverse events occurred less frequently in the cagrilintide group than in the semaglutide and cagrilintide–semaglutide groups. Treatment discontinuation due to adverse events was similar to that in other studies of incretin-based therapies. The efficacy and safety of cagrilintide–semaglutide in persons with type 2 diabetes and overweight or obesity are being assessed in the REDEFINE 2 trial

Cagrilintide-semaglutide in adults with overweight or obesity and type 2 diabetes
